perturbation.original_text: Custom er agrees that, without the prior written consent of Minnesota Power, it will not m ake any statem ent to any com petitor, cust omer or other third party with respect to the Inform ation exchanged hereunder or regarding the Project itself.
perturbation.changed_text: Custom er agrees that, without the consent of Minnesota Power, it will not make any statement to any third party with respect to the Information exchanged hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'competitor, customer' and 'regarding the project itself' and 'written' weakens the scope and enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement. This creates legal risk as it can be interpreted as a restriction on free speech or disparagement, which are protected to some extent under the First Amendment and further delineated in Minnesota law. A written consent provides a clear record of authorization, while oral consent can be difficult to prove.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text has a narrower scope by specifically mentioning competitors and customers, focusing on commercially sensitive statements and requiring written consent from Minnesota Power for statements about the information or the project. The modified text removes the written requirement, and by broadening the prohibition to 'any statement to any third party' and removing the scope of 'the project itself,' it could be interpreted as an overly broad restriction on speech, potentially conflicting with the freedom of speech protections in the Minnesota Constitution, Article 1, Section 3, and the general principle that restraints on speech must be narrowly tailored. The omission creates ambiguity and makes the clause more vulnerable to legal challenge.

Minnesota Constitution From Ballotpedia Jump to: navigation , search Minnesota Constitution Preamble Articles I • II • III • IV • V • VI • VII • VIII • IX • X • XI • XII • XIII • XIV The Minnesota Constitution is the state constitution of Minnesota . The current Minnesota Constitution was adopted on October 13, 1857. [1] Minnesota has had one state constitution. The Minnesota Constitution has 14 articles. The current constitution has been amended 121 times . [2] Voters last approved a new amendment to the Minnesota Constitution on November 5, 2024 , when voters approved Amendment 1 . Minnesota does not feature a process for initiated constitutional amendments . Thus, amendments in Minnesota can be put on the ballot through referral by the legislature or by a constitutional convention . A state constitution is the fundamental document that outlines a state's framework for governance, including the powers, structure, and limitations of the state government, individual and civil rights, and other matters. Contents 1 Background 2 Preamble 3 Article I 4 Article II 5 Article III 6 Article IV 7 Article V 8 Article VI 9 Article VII 10 Article VIII 11 Article IX 12 Article X 13 Article XI 14 Article XII 15 Article XIII 16 Article XIV 17 Amending the constitution 17.1 Legislature 17.2 Convention 18 See also 19 External links 20 Footnotes Background Minnesota became the 32nd state on May 11, 1858. The Minnesota Constitution was adopted on October 13, 1857. In 1971, the Minnesota State Legislature established a constitutional study commission. After studying the state's 1857 constitution, the commission recommended it be restructured for easy reference and rewritten in modern language. An amendment to revise the constitution was passed by the state legislature and ratified by voters on November 5, 1974. [3] Preamble See also: Preamble, Minnesota Constitution and Preambles to state constitutions The preamble to the constitution states: We, the people of the state of Minnesota, grateful to God for our civil and religious liberty, and desiring to perpetuate its blessings and secure the same to ourselves and our posterity, do ordain and establish this Constitution. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the non-disclosure agreement by removing 'competitor, customer' and 'regarding the project itself,' and 'written' from 'written consent'. The law explanation states this broadened prohibition to 'any statement to any third party' and removing the scope of 'the project itself,' could be interpreted as an overly broad restriction on speech, potentially conflicting with the freedom of speech protections in the Minnesota Constitution. The absence of 'written' consent makes the agreement difficult to enforce. The scraped law snippet 2 mentions 'Minnesota Constitution' and 'Article I', which is titled 'Bill of Rights'. The altered text makes the clause more vulnerable to legal challenge, therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure or use of the Information shall remain in full force and effect unless otherwise agreed to by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific term for how long the confidentiality provisions survive termination of the agreement, and removing the qualifier that the agreement to change the terms must be in writing, the contract introduces ambiguity and legal risk. This contradicts the principle of definiteness in contract law, as it leaves the duration of the confidentiality obligations uncertain. Minnesota law, like most jurisdictions, requires contracts to have reasonably certain terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text establishes a clear duration for the confidentiality obligations, either for a specific number of years or as long as the information remains confidential. Removing this specific term and the requirement for a written agreement creates uncertainty regarding the duration of the confidentiality obligations and contradicts Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33, which states that even if the terms are reasonably certain, an offer cannot be accepted so as to form a contract unless the terms of the contract are reasonably certain. Removing that degree of clarity, makes the contract potentially unenforceable under Minnesota law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute A statute is a law enacted by a legislature. Statutes are also called acts, such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 or the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . Federal laws must be passed by both houses of Congress, the House of Representative and the Senate, and then usually require approval from the President before they can take effect. As explained by the Library of Congress , enacted federal statutes are published multiple times. First, each individual law is published as a “slip law.” Then, all of the slip laws for each session of Congress are published together as “session laws.” Finally, all laws that are of a “general and permanent nature” are eventually compiled into the United States Code , and also the Revised Statutes of the United States. State statutes can be found through the LII's page on Constitutions, Statutes, and Codes . Readers can also attempt to find state statutes organized by topic: Topical Index: State Statutes . [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS wex definitions government legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a duration for nondisclosure obligations, either a set number of years or as long as the information remains confidential. The changed text removes this specific duration and the requirement for a written agreement to alter the terms. This introduces ambiguity regarding how long the confidentiality obligations remain in effect, contradicting the principle of definiteness in contract law, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which references Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33. The absence of clear terms could make the contract unenforceable under Minnesota law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error, Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'statute' as a law enacted by a legislature, reinforcing the legal weight that statutes carry and emphasizing the importance of adherence to legal standards in contractual agreements.
